sentences.text: Mei was three years old when she knew she was different.
It is her first day at preschool in Toronto.
All the other children are white and play together.
They all spoke English and understood each other well.
Mei has dark hair and brown eyes.
She spoke Cantonese at home with her grandpa and felt happy.
When her grandfather tried to leave, Mei got very upset.
She screamed and cried while holding onto his leg.
She knew she would be alone if he left.
Mei feels alone because of her difference.
Her grandfather stayed in Toronto and visited Mei.
He stayed at preschool with her every day for two years.
He cannot speak any English words.
He was there but did not speak.
Mei is scared to be alone in a place that feels strange.
Mei's parents moved to Canada before she was born.
They came from a small village in China and had little money.
They meet in English class.
They married in a small office and saved money for the future.
They both work full time and are very busy.
Her mother cleans office buildings at night and works hard.
Her father worked in a restaurant kitchen during the day.
They left when Mei did not wake up.
They came home when she was asleep.
Her grandfather takes care of Mei and her little brother.
When Mei started school, she had only two friends.
Their names are Amy and Catherine.
They were the only other Asian kids in her whole grade.
Mei feels safe when she is with them.
They understood the problem and found a solution.
But she does not feel comfortable with them.
She felt they knew something about being Chinese that she did not.
They spoke Mandarin and Cantonese at home in China.
Mei's parents did not want to teach her.
They wanted her to pay attention to English.
Mei wants to fit in with her friends in Toronto.
To succeed in Canada, you need hard work and good friends.
At school, Mei watched the other children and smiled.
She saw what they wore.
They ate lunch together and enjoyed their food.
They talked in a nice way and shared their thoughts.
She wanted to copy them well and be perfect.
When her mother said to pack rice and vegetables, Mei said no.
She asked for sandwiches and wanted to eat them.
Mei eats plain sandwiches with cheese and peanut butter.
Nothing smelled different from what Mei knew.
There is nothing to make the other children look and stare.
At home, Mei saw her parents change a lot.
They spoke in normal voices at the dinner table.
They laughed loudly and had fun together.
They tell stories in Cantonese and move their hands quickly.
When the phone rang, it was her father's boss and everything changed.
Her father’s voice is quiet now.
It is softer than before and feels nice.
Be more careful and pay attention to details.
He used easier words to help people understand.
He said sorry for things that were not his fault.
When he hung up, Mei asked why he talked like that.
He said it is easier.
Mei saw the shame in his eyes and felt sad.
One day, Mei went with her parents to buy a washing machine.
The salesman looked at Mei's parents and then looked away.
He talked to Mei instead of someone else.
He asked her what her parents want.
He asked her if they understand him.
Mei felt hot on her face.
She felt embarrassed and did not want to talk.
She does not do it for herself.
It is for her parents in Toronto, Canada.
They stand right there and watch.
They spoke English, so they understood each other well.
The salesman treats them like they are not there.
Her mother sometimes wears traditional clothing from China.
Long dresses have beautiful embroidery and look very nice.
When they went out, people looked at them.
Mei started to hate those dresses and felt very unhappy.
She wanted her mother to wear jeans and T-shirts like others.
She wanted her parents to speak clearly and without accents.
She wanted them to fit in and feel good.
She did not blend in when they did not blend in.
In middle school, Mei made a choice and felt happy.
She chose to stay away from things that made her different.
She stopped speaking Cantonese and forgot the little she knew.
She does not want to go to Chinese school on Saturdays.
She spent all her time with her friends in Canada.
She joined the volleyball team and felt happy.
Mei got good grades in English but stopped trying in math.
Her friends call her whitewashed because she is different now.
Mei felt proud when they said they don’t see her as Asian.
She calls herself a banana when she feels funny.
It is yellow on the outside.
It is white inside and looks nice.
It was a funny joke and everyone laughed.
But it was also the truth of what Mei wanted to be.
When Mei was thirteen, she delivered newspapers to earn money.
She puts a paper in a mailbox when a man comes out.
He shook his head at her and looked unhappy.
Mei asked politely if you want the paper.
The man shook his head and waved her away.
The next week, an old woman waits for Mei at the house.
The woman said he does not want his paper delivered.
He does not like people from China.
Mei stands frozen but still wants it.
She felt pain in her stomach like a punch.
She wanted to cry because she felt very sad.
She felt angry and more than that.
Mei is angry at the man.
Mei is angry at herself because she made a mistake.
She tries hard to be different from who she is.
It did not matter at all to me.
To that man, she is always Chinese and proud.
She is always yellow on the outside and feels different.
It did not matter what color she was inside or outside.
Mei took a history class in high school.
They studied how people moved to Canada in the 1920s.
The textbook showed old cartoons from newspapers.
They showed Chinese people as bad and not good.
It is a danger to people and the world.
They showed signs that said "No Chinese Allowed" and the teacher talked about pay.
They were blamed for taking jobs in Canada and China.
They were hated by many people in China.
Mei looked at the cartoons and felt very sick.
She thinks about the newspaper man who does not like Chinese people.
Mei thought about her father and his boss on the phone.
She thought about the salesman who did not see her parents.
She saw that nothing really changed for her.
The hate is quieter now but still there.
She is more polite than before.
But she is still there.
She looked around her classroom and saw many history books.
She was the only Asian student in her class.
All her white friends sigh and shake their heads at racism.
They did not see that it was still going on.
It is right in front of them.
I write a letter to Mei in Toronto.
She talks to her parents in Cantonese.
Mei goes to school every day.
That night, Mei went home and looked in the glass.
She spent years trying to be less Chinese and fit in.
It is less different than before.
She looks at her face and knows the truth.
She will always be Chinese and proud of it.
Her English was good, but it was not perfect.
She had many white friends but felt alone sometimes.
I will help you no matter what happens.
She thinks about her grandfather and misses him a lot.
He stayed at preschool with her every day for two years.
He never learned English but stayed true to himself.
She thinks about her parents and misses them very much.
They worked hard to give Mei good chances in life.
They gave up everything and felt very sad.
Their language is Cantonese and it is spoken in China.
Their home is in Toronto, Canada, but they come from China.
Their culture is rich and comes from China and Canada.
Everything is for her and she is happy.
She felt ashamed and did not want to show it.
None of them are here.
She thinks about herself and her life in Canada.
When Mei went to university, her life changed a lot.
On her first day, Mei sits alone when students come to her.
They asked her if she wants to sit with them.
At first, Mei wants to say no but changes her mind.
Old habits are hard to change and can stay with us.
But something makes her say yes.
They talked about their families and shared their stories.
Mei grows up in Toronto and feels caught between two worlds.
Mei feels too white for her Asian family and too Asian for friends.
They felt shame about their parents' accents and their mothers' food.
They saw the racism but pretended not to see it.
They laughed at the jokes even when they hurt.
For the first time, Mei did not need to explain herself.
Everyone at the table understands what is happening.
They all lived it before and remember it well.
They all carry the same weight and feel it together.
They feel confused about where they belong in Canada.
One girl said she called herself a banana before.
I felt proud, but everyone laughed at me.
They all did the same thing and felt the same way.
They all tried to change parts of themselves and be better.
They could not erase parts no matter how hard they tried.
Mei spends more time with her friends now.
Mei asked her parents to teach her Cantonese again.
Mei went to the library and got books about China.
She signed up for a Mandarin class in Toronto.
She cooked traditional foods with her mother in China.
Mei bought her mother flowers and said the dresses were beautiful.
Her mother cried because she felt very sad.
In her second year, Mei gives a presentation for art class.
When it was her turn, Mei stood up in front of everyone.
For the first time, she introduces herself the right way.
She said, "My name is Mei.
It is not the way Mei always says it.
But this is the real way.
The way in China is very different from Canada.
Mei remembers how her grandfather always said it.
Some people in the class look confused and worried.
Mei did not care about anything at all.
She claims something that always belongs to her.
Her name is Mei and she is from China.
Her identity is important to her and connects to her culture.
Her place in the world is important and makes her happy.
After class, a white student talks to her.
He said he could not say your name before.
Mei smiled and asked, "Can you teach me the right way.
She realized something important that day in Toronto.
She does not have to choose being Chinese and being Canadian.
She did not have to choose one or the other.
She can be both happy and sad.
She can be something new and exciting.
There is something else important to say.
A bridge connects two different worlds in life.
She would never feel like she belongs in China.
She is born in Canada and lives in Toronto.
She thinks in English.
She never lived in her parents' village in China.
But she will never fully belong in the Canadian world.
Her face always shows that she is different.
Her family history is always different from others.
That was fine and everything was okay.
She creates her own culture in Toronto, Canada, and feels proud.
A third culture is a mix of different ways of life.
It took the best things from both worlds and made them better.
Mei loves the strength and sacrifice of her parents.
Canada has independence and many opportunities for people.
The food, language, and history of China are very rich.
Toronto is diverse and open to many cultures.
She was not yellow outside and white inside.
She is not a banana now.
Her name is Mei and she is from China.
Mei is fully Chinese and speaks Cantonese at home.
Mei is fully Canadian and lives in Toronto, Canada.
She is fully herself and feels happy.
When she went home, she asked her father for stories from China.
He looked surprised when he saw the news.
His eyes fill with tears when he feels sad.
He starts to talk and shares his ideas.
Cantonese and English mix together in one conversation.
Mei thinks about rice fields and his grandfather when he leaves home.
Mei listened to music and felt happy.
I really listened to what Mei said.
She felt something new that she had not felt before.
Pride is a strong feeling of happiness and respect for oneself.
There is no shame in being yourself and feeling good.
There is no embarrassment for Mei in Toronto, Canada.
Mei feels deep pride in where she comes from.
Mei wants to know who her parents are.
She thinks about who she is becoming.
Her mother came and sat down next to her.
She brings tea and almond cookies.
The three of them sat together when the sun set.
Two languages mix in the air around Mei.
Two cultures meet in the same room and share ideas.
They are all in the same family.
In the same person, there are many good and bad things.
Mei finally understands what her grandfather knew all along.
You do not need to make a choice.
You do not need to change who you are to fit in.
You are your own person and you belong to yourself.
That is enough for me and I feel happy.
